ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for spicy sour beef rice noodle set specifically; mapped to broader 'noodle dish' class (FOODON_00002473) as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FJP (rice noodles, cooked) used as the closest defensible anchor for this composite noodle set dish; no specific code exists for the full set with beef and pickled vegetables.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific composite dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.description: 1 set meal (noodle bowl + drink + pickled radish side)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard set meal portion is approximately 550 g, comprising a bowl of spicy sour beef rice noodles (roughly 400 g), a side of pickled radish (approximately 50 g), and a choice of drink (approximately 100 g).

nutrient_source.title: Spicy and Sour Beef Rice Noodle Set — HPB Singapore Foodo Database (G00010497)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database record G00010497 for this dish. All per-100g values are taken as reported. Saturated fat is not separately reported in the HPB record; estimated from USDA FoodData Central beef and rice noodle component data as a supplementary calculation. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and several micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice noodles, cooked (analogous estimate)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for this composite set meal. GI of 61 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked rice noodles (GI ~61, medium category), which is the dominant carbohydrate-contributing ingredient. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (61) × available carbohydrate per serving (25.19 g/100g × 550 g serving × 0.9 noodle fraction / 100) ≈ 33.8. The spicy sour broth with vinegar may modestly reduce postprandial glucose response, but this effect is not quantified in the GI estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100 g; full set meal estimated at ~1,270 mg sodium per serving, approaching the 1,500 mg daily DASH target in a single meal
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced broth volume and avoid adding soy sauce or chili condiments, as the spicy sour broth is the primary sodium source; the favourable potassium-to-sodium ratio (327 mg K : 231 mg Na per 100 g) partially offsets vascular risk but does not eliminate it at full-serving quantities.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Rice noodles GI ~61 (medium); carbohydrate 25.19 g/100 g with dietary fibre 3.7 g/100 g moderating glycaemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion size to approximately 300 g of the noodle component, leverage the fibre content (3.7 g/100 g) and vinegar-based broth to moderate postprandial glucose, and verify that no added sugar is present in the sour sauce; blood glucose monitoring after consumption is advised given the medium GI and high carbohydrate load per full serving.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Fatty beef (肥牛) contributes saturated fat (~1.6 g/100 g estimated); sodium load at full serving is clinically significant for CVD risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should request leaner beef substitutes where possible, consume a reduced portion of the broth to limit sodium intake, and note that the dish's iron content (2.55 mg/100 g) from red meat may be beneficial for anaemia-related CVD comorbidities, but the saturated fat and sodium profile warrants moderation.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Fatty beef (肥牛) is high in arachidonic acid (omega-6) and saturated fat, amplifying inflammatory pathways via TNF-alpha and IL-6
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish in its standard form due to the fatty beef component; substituting with chicken breast or tofu and reducing chili (a nightshade ingredient) is strongly recommended to minimise joint inflammation risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spicy and Sour Beef Rice Noodle Set (酸辣肥牛米线套餐) is a popular Chinese-style noodle set meal served in Singapore, featuring a bowl of rice noodles (米线, mǐ xiàn) submerged in a tangy, spicy broth made with pickled vegetables (酸菜, suān cài), chili, and vinegar. The set includes thinly sliced fatty beef (肥牛, féi niú) that is briefly cooked in the hot broth, a side of crunchy pickled radish (爽口脆萝卜), and a choice of drink. The dish delivers a complex flavour profile: sour from the pickled vegetables and vinegar, spicy from chili, savoury from the broth seasoning, and rich from the fatty beef. The rice noodles are smooth and silky in texture, while the pickled radish adds a refreshing crunch. Nutritionally, the dish provides 164 kcal per 100 g, with 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, and 25.19 g carbohydrate. Dietary fibre at 3.7 g/100 g is notable and contributes to moderate glycaemic response. Sodium at 231 mg/100 g is a clinical consideration for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients, particularly at full serving sizes. The dish is classified as medium GI (~61) based on the rice noodle component. It is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans due to the beef content, and individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should exercise caution due to the pro-inflammatory fatty beef. The vinegar-based sour broth may confer modest postprandial glucose-moderating benefits. This dish is commonly consumed at lunch or dinner in Singapore's Chinese food establishments.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (G00010497) with confidence 0.95 as reported in the input record. The dish is a canonical Singapore Chinese restaurant item with a stable ingredient profile. Saturated fat is estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data (medium confidence). GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for cooked rice noodles.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.6 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central data for cooked rice noodles and fatty beef slices in proportion to the dish composition, as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this record. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this composite dish not available in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 3. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient and moisture data for this composite set meal not reported in HPB Foodo database record G00010497 and not available in USDA FoodData Central for this specific dish formulation as of 2026. 4. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 in the HPB source record; this is accepted as confirmed zero per the approved source, noting that fatty beef typically contains cholesterol and this value may reflect a measurement artefact or broth-dilution effect — consumers should be aware. 5. GI of 61 is an analogous estimate based on cooked rice noodles from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this composite set meal exists in the approved GI literature. 6. Halal status is not confirmed; consumers should verify with the specific vendor. 7. The drink component (one of three choices) is not nutritionally specified in the HPB record; nutrient values reflect the noodle set as a whole per HPB reporting.
